notes: v1.3: added a 3rd adversarial_threat_modeling question (q_adversarial_3) after a real user's Security Engineer match landed exactly at the scoring floor (50%) because both existing questions' anchor-3 options ('a few obvious abuse cases,' 'sometimes, as part of general engineering diligence') describe ordinary responsible-engineer behavior that most conscientious engineers would honestly claim, regardless of any real draw toward security work — a 'safe middle answer' problem, not a scoring bug. The new question asks about actual behavioral history (sought-out security work, CTFs, bug bounties) rather than an abstract frequency self-rating, which is harder to safely answer in the middle. adversarial_threat_modeling is now averaged over 3 questions instead of 2 (every other dimension still has 2, except v1.2's domain-fluency dimensions which have 1). v1.2: added q_public_visibility_1 (new public_visibility_comfort dimension, split out of teaching_enjoyment) and 4 new domain-fluency questions (q_ml_fluency_1, q_mobile_fluency_1, q_data_fluency_1, q_cloud_fluency_1) for ml_engineering_fluency/mobile_platform_fluency/data_infrastructure_fluency/cloud_infrastructure_fluency, each with only 1 question (not 2) to limit assessment-length growth — see docs/research/dimensions-v1.2-teaching-split-and-domain-fluency.md. v1.1: replaced q_account_1/q_account_2 (old account_relationship_ownership dimension) with q_breadth_1/q_breadth_2 (account_portfolio_breadth) and q_continuity_1/q_continuity_2 (relationship_continuity) per the v1.1 dimension split — see docs/research/dimensions-v1.1-relationship-split.md. Each dimension question's `value` options span the dimension's 1-5 scale; a user's final U[d] fed into taxonomy/scoring.md is the average of their answered questions for that dimension (nulls excluded per scoring.md's skip handling). `section` matches docs/assessment/flow.md's four sections.

stackIntake.notes: Fast structured input, not scored against the 16 trait dimensions directly — used to personalize result copy and lightly qualify technical_breadth_depth/coding_intensity self-reports against a sanity check. Deliberately short per PLAN.md Phase 3 spec.

fields.label: How many years have you been working as a software engineer (or equivalent)?
Which languages do you use most?
Which of these describe work you've done? (select all that apply)
What's your current (or most recent) job title?

questions.prompt: Your manager says: “We think there's an opportunity here, but we don't have a clear spec — go figure out what we should build.” How do you honestly feel?

options.label: I'd want at least a rough spec before starting — this feels like a setup for wasted work.
I'd dig in, but I'd want to agree on a rough goal with my manager first.
I'd be energized — defining the problem myself is half the fun.

questions.prompt: Your team's goal for a project is described only as “make customers happier,” with no agreed metric for what that means. Honest reaction?

options.label: Frustrating — I want a number to aim at before I start.
Workable, but I'd push hard to define a metric before executing.
Fine by me — I'll figure out how to tell if it worked as I go.

questions.prompt: It's 10am and you had two hours blocked for deep work, but three “quick question” Slack messages come in. What do you do?

options.label: Ignore them until my blocked time is over — deep work comes first.
Glance at them, answer the truly quick one, defer the rest.
Answer all three right away — I like the variety and the momentum.

questions.prompt: Which describes your ideal workday cadence best?

options.label: Long stretches of self-scheduled focus time, few unplanned check-ins.
A mix of planned meetings and occasional interruptions I can usually predict.
A stream of live requests I respond to as they come in — that's the job.

questions.prompt: Your phone buzzes at 2am: production is down. How do you feel about that possibility being a real part of your job?

options.label: A dealbreaker — I don't want to be woken up for work.
Fine as part of a fair rotation, but I wouldn't seek it out.
Bring it on — I like the pressure and the payoff of fixing it live.

questions.prompt: A serious incident happens on a system you're responsible for, outside work hours. Your gut reaction?

options.label: I'd want someone else to own that call entirely.
I'd help if truly necessary, but I'd hope it's rare.
I'd want to be the one leading the response.

questions.prompt: When something breaks and the cause isn't obvious, which describes you best?

options.label: I can usually trace it from application logs pretty quickly.
I can dig into logs, third-party services, or infra config, given time to research.
I can form and test hypotheses across totally different failure domains under real time pressure.

questions.prompt: Think of the hardest bug you've personally solved. Which is closest to what happened?

options.label: A clear stack trace pointed me right at the fix.
I had to check a couple of systems/services before finding the real cause.
The cause spanned multiple totally different systems, and I had to rule out several wrong theories first.

questions.prompt: How would you describe the systems you've designed?

options.label: Well-specified components that fit a defined spec.
Services or schemas that work well for my team's current scale.
Platforms meant to serve many teams or scale far beyond current load.

questions.prompt: When you design something, how far ahead do you typically think?

options.label: Whether it solves the immediate problem correctly.
Whether it'll hold up for my team over the next year or so.
Whether it'll hold up as the org and traffic grow by 10x, for teams I'll never talk to.

questions.prompt: Where do you naturally sit?

scale.minLabel: Deep specialist in one narrow layer or platform
scale.maxLabel: Fluid across many stacks/platforms — I prefer variety

questions.prompt: How many different technologies or platforms are you comfortable being thrown into on short notice?

scale.minLabel: One — I go deep and stay there
scale.maxLabel: Any — I'll pick up whatever the situation needs

questions.prompt: A metric you're responsible for goes bad, partly due to factors outside your control. How do you feel about being the named owner of that?

options.label: Uncomfortable — I shouldn't be blamed for things I don't control.
OK, as long as there's fair attribution before real blame lands.
Fine — I'll own it and focus on the fix, regardless of the cause.

questions.prompt: What matters more to you, day to day?

options.label: That the system/design is well-built and technically sound.
Both — sound systems that also produce results.
That it drives the actual business/user outcome, even if the tech underneath isn't elegant.

questions.prompt: You're in a room with a skeptical senior engineer, a procurement person, and an exec — all at once, and you need all three to trust you. Reaction?

options.label: I'd rather email each of them separately.
Doable if I've prepared, but not my favorite setting.
This is exactly the kind of moment I'm good at.

questions.prompt: Someone from another team (or a client) treats you like a vendor they need convincing to use. Honest reaction?

options.label: Frustrating — I'd rather just be told what to build.
Fair enough, I'll make the case if I have to.
I like this dynamic — earning adoption is part of the job.

questions.prompt: You've just solved a tricky problem. Someone asks you to turn it into a tutorial for people who've never seen it before. Reaction?

options.label: I'd rather just move on to the next problem.
I'll do it if it's expected, and I don't mind it.
I'd genuinely enjoy this — teaching it well is its own reward.

questions.prompt: A customer doesn't understand something you explained clearly to a peer five minutes ago. What now?

options.label: Frustrating — I already explained it once.
I'll rephrase and try again — it's part of the job.
I enjoy finding the explanation that finally clicks for them.

questions.prompt: Someone suggests you give a talk about your work at a public meetup or conference. Honest reaction?

options.label: Hard pass — that's not something I want any part of.
I could do it once in a while, but I wouldn't seek it out.
I'd genuinely want to do this regularly — being known publicly for what I do sounds appealing, not draining.

questions.prompt: Which sounds more like you?

options.label: One account or project at a time — full focus until it's done.
A handful of things going at once — enough to stay interesting without feeling scattered.
A large portfolio of many things running in parallel — I like the pace of juggling a lot at once.

questions.prompt: Your manager can give you one deep engagement to own for months, or a rotating set of 5-6 smaller ones at once. Which do you pick?

options.label: The one deep engagement, no question.
Either is fine — depends on the specifics.
The rotating set — I like having several things moving at once.

questions.prompt: How do you feel about picking up “whoever's next in the queue,” with no history or context?

options.label: I actually prefer this — fresh problem, no baggage.
It's fine — not my preference, but workable.
I'd rather have continuity — knowing someone's whole history feels more meaningful.

questions.prompt: A client or account you worked with wraps up, and you're told you probably won't work with them again. Reaction?

options.label: Totally fine — on to the next one.
A little bittersweet, but that's how the job works.
That's the part of a job I'd like least — I want relationships that keep going.

questions.prompt: You're offered a role where 30% of your pay depends on hitting a quarterly number you don't fully control. Reaction?

options.label: No thanks — I want my pay decoupled from things outside my control.
I could live with a modest bonus tied to a soft proxy, not a hard sales number.
I want that upside — bring on the risk/reward.

questions.prompt: How do you feel about your income varying meaningfully quarter to quarter based on performance?

options.label: I want predictable, stable pay.
Some variability is fine if it's modest.
I'd actively choose more variable upside over a flatter salary.

questions.prompt: How much of your ideal week is spent personally writing/reviewing/debugging the code that ships?

options.label: Little to none — I'd rather review, advise, or direct others' work.
Some — enough to prototype or validate, but not the majority of my time.
Most of it — shipping code I wrote is the main thing I do.

questions.prompt: Roughly what share of your time this past year was (or would be) spent hands-on-keyboard writing production code?
How do you feel about travel for work?

scale.minLabel: Working from one place is important — travel is a dealbreaker
scale.maxLabel: I'm happy being on the road or embedded at a client site most weeks

questions.prompt: Picture being physically embedded at a client's office for weeks at a time. Reaction?

options.label: Not for me.
I could do this occasionally.
I'd genuinely enjoy this rhythm.

questions.prompt: You're reviewing a new feature before launch. What's your first instinct?

options.label: Does it work correctly for the intended use case?
Does it work correctly, and are there a few obvious abuse cases to check?
How would someone deliberately try to break or exploit this?

questions.prompt: How often do you find yourself thinking about how a system could be attacked, not just how it could fail by accident?

options.label: Rarely, if ever.
Sometimes, as part of general engineering diligence.
Constantly — it's my default lens on any system.

questions.prompt: Which is closer to your actual work history, not just how you'd like to see yourself?

options.label: I've never sought out security-focused work, reviews, or assignments — it's not something I gravitate toward.
I've done security-adjacent work when it landed on my plate, but haven't gone looking for it.
I've actively sought out security-focused work — a CTF, bug bounty, a security review rotation, or a dedicated security role or project.

questions.prompt: How do you feel about hard physical limits (power, memory, real-time deadlines) as pass/fail constraints rather than nice-to-haves?

scale.minLabel: Not part of my work at all
scale.maxLabel: I treat these as absolute gates and debug at the hardware/software boundary

questions.prompt: Have you used tools like an oscilloscope, logic analyzer, or JTAG debugger as a normal part of your work?

options.label: No, and I don't expect to.
Not really, but I'm comfortable with CI-tracked performance budgets.
Yes, regularly, as part of my normal debugging process.

questions.prompt: How much hands-on experience do you have building, training, or deploying machine learning systems?

options.label: None — machine learning hasn't been part of what I've built.
Some — I've used ML APIs/libraries or built a model or pipeline component.
A lot — I've built, trained, deployed, or maintained production ML systems as a core part of my work.

questions.prompt: How much hands-on experience do you have building native or cross-platform mobile apps (iOS/Android)?

options.label: None — I haven't built mobile apps.
Some — I've built a small app or contributed to a mobile codebase.
A lot — I've built and shipped mobile apps as a core part of my work.

questions.prompt: How much hands-on experience do you have building or operating data pipelines or warehouse infrastructure?

options.label: None — I haven't built data pipelines or warehouse infrastructure.
Some — I've written ETL/ELT jobs or maintained a warehouse.
A lot — I've built and operated production data infrastructure as a core part of my work.

questions.prompt: How much hands-on experience do you have building or operating cloud infrastructure or CI/CD systems?

options.label: None — I haven't built or operated cloud/infra systems.
Some — I've configured cloud resources or CI/CD for my own team's use.
A lot — I've built and operated cloud infrastructure or internal platforms as a core part of my work.

questions.prompt: Imagine your performance review next year is based entirely on your team's retention, morale, and growth — not anything you personally built. Reaction?

options.label: That sounds like a distraction from real work.
I could do this, and I'd take it seriously, but I'd miss building things myself.
That's exactly the kind of success I want to be judged on.

questions.prompt: A teammate is underperforming and it's affecting the team's mood. Handling this kind of situation, honestly:

options.label: I'd rather someone else deal with this entirely.
I'd handle it if it were my job, but it's draining.
I'd want to be the one who works through this — it's part of what I find meaningful.
